messages.content: لطفا متن زیر را به زبان انگلیسی به گونه ایی بازنویسی کن که معنی و مفهوم آن حفظ شود و تغییر نکند. Opioids, including morphine and fentanyl, used for maintenance of analgesic effects in neuraxial anesthesia are accompanied with complications like nausea, vomiting, pruritus and respiratory depression. Tramadol is a weak opioid and without respiratory depression. However, its intrathecal prescription simultaneously with other opioids such as morphine needs vigilant monitoring for early detection of any respiratory depression incidence through routine methods.

Please rewrite the following text in English while maintaining its meaning and concept unchanged: Opioids, such as morphine and fentanyl, commonly administered for pain relief during neuraxial anesthesia, may cause complications such as nausea, vomiting, itching, and respiratory depression. Tramadol, a less potent opioid, does not typically lead to respiratory depression. However, if it is prescribed intrathecally alongside other opioids like morphine, careful monitoring is essential to promptly detect any signs of respiratory depression using routine methods.

Opioids (opiates) are a class of analgesics extensively used for pain treatment in medicine. Pain relief or euphoria are the main effects of opioids as a class of controlled and illicit medications (narcotics). Opioid dependence and abuse are an epidemic, as revealed by a dramatic rise in fatal overdoses.

Opioids, also known as opiates, are a widely utilized group of pain medications in the medical field. The main effects of opioids include pain relief and a feeling of euphoria. However, it is important to note that opioids are controlled substances and their misuse can lead to dependence and abuse. Sadly, the rise in fatal overdoses indicates an epidemic of opioid addiction.

Tramadol acts by binding to μ-opioid receptors on neurons and inhibits the reuptake of both norepinephrine and serotonin neurotransmitters. It stimulates the dopamine receptors and also inhibits the gamma amino butyric acid release in central nervous system.

Tramadol exerts its effects by binding to μ-opioid receptors found on neurons, which results in the inhibition of the reuptake of both norepinephrine and serotonin neurotransmitters. Additionally, it stimulates dopamine receptors and inhibits the release of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) in the central nervous system.
lthough tramadol is an anti-addictive drug, the strong dependence of addicts on it can be attributed to its influence on opioid receptors in the central nervous system (CNS).

While tramadol is considered to be an anti-addictive medication, the strong dependence observed in individuals with addiction issues can be attributed to its effects on the opioid receptors in the central nervous system (CNS).
It was found that tramadol is highly effective for relief of post operative pain, moderate surgical pain, cancer pain control, and chronic pain, and treatment of rheumatoid arthritis.

Tramadol has been found to be highly effective in providing relief for postoperative pain, moderate surgical pain, controlling pain in cancer patients, managing chronic pain, and treating rheumatoid arthritis.
Tramadol overdose can cause vomiting, problems in the CNS and respiratory system, dizziness, seizures, depression, nausea, tachycardia, and coma

An overdose of tramadol can result in various symptoms such as vomiting, disturbances in the central nervous system (CNS) and respiratory system, dizziness, seizures, depression, nausea, rapid heartbeat (tachycardia), and even coma.
It is widely accepted that morphine is one of the phenolic compounds as well as an alkaloid that is able to disrupt the central nervous system.
It is widely recognized that morphine is classified as both a phenolic compound and an alkaloid, capable of affecting the central nervous system.
Morphine interacts predominantly with the μ-opioid receptor. These μ-binding sites are discretely distributed in the human brain.
Morphine primarily interacts with the μ-opioid receptor, which is found in discrete distributions throughout the human brain.
Morphine interacts predominantly with the μ-opioid receptor. These μ-binding sites are discretely distributed in the human brain. They are also found on the terminal axons of primary afferents.

Morphine primarily interacts with the μ-opioid receptor, which is predominantly distributed throughout the human brain. These μ-binding sites are also present on the terminal axons of primary afferents.

Morphine is a strong opioid used for treatment of moderate to severe pain, especially in chronic cancer pain. Unfortunately, morphine like all opioids, can also cause addiction, overdose, and even death due to respiratory depression.

Morphine is a potent opioid medication commonly prescribed for the management of moderate to severe pain, particularly in cases of chronic cancer pain. However, it is important to note that like all opioids, morphine carries the risk of addiction, overdose, and potentially fatal respiratory depression.

Accordingly, experts in forensic and clinical fields, including monitoring of drug abuse, diagnosis of poisoning and evaluating of medicolegal death, focused on finding sensitive approaches to detect morphine in biological fluids such as urine.

Therefore, experts in forensic and clinical fields, who specialize in areas such as drug abuse monitoring, poisoning diagnosis, and medicolegal death evaluation, have placed significant emphasis on developing sensitive methods for detecting morphine in biological fluids, such as urine.
Several techniques have been reported in the scientific database for the determination of these two materials in pharmaceutical and biological fluids simultaneously or individually.
Various techniques have been documented in the scientific literature for the simultaneous or individual determination of both morphine and its metabolites in pharmaceutical and biological fluids.

Gas chromatography coupled to mass spectrometry (GC–MS)
[18], Spectrophotometry [19], chemiluminescence [20], high–
performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [21],
spectrofluorimetry [22], and surface plasmon resonance [23],
have been employed for this purpose.

Various techniques, including gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (GC-MS), spectrophotometry, chemiluminescence, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), spectrofluorimetry, and surface plasmon resonance, have been utilized for the determination of morphine and its metabolites in pharmaceutical and biological fluids [18-23].
However, many of these approaches are expensive and laborious, demand complicated sample pre-treatment phases, requires professional personnel to run, and takes up to several hours to complete.

However, it is worth noting that many of these methods can be expensive, time-consuming, involve complex sample pre-treatment steps, require skilled personnel for operation, and may take several hours to complete.
Thus, designing a simplified, cost-effective, sensitive, and precise analytical procedure would be favorable for the detection of methadone and morphine.
Therefore, the development of a simplified, cost-effective, sensitive, and precise analytical procedure would be highly desirable for the detection of both methadone and morphine.
